content:
Yet as Adams turns 80, the city can do a little more to celebrate his legacy

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

California-based composer John Adams [Image credit: Vern Evans]Being a San Franciscan today is no easy thing. The AI revolution — much of it housed in Silicon Valley — is displacing workers at precipitous rates, with more than 100,000 tech layoffs since the start of this year alone. San Francisco also recorded the second-highest number of fatal drug overdoses in 2025, topped only by Baltimore.
Moreover, San Francisco’s arts and cultural scene is being gutted from the inside, with the California Arts Council budget hovering around a paltry $21 million. This places the state at #35 on the national ranking of per-capita arts funding, well behind Florida and Tennessee.
There is uncertainty whether the state will survive as one which fights for the things that make life worth living — and yes, that is the arts as well as job security — or one that gradually gives up on them.
There is a decent test case right now to support the latter. Composer John Adams is one of the most influential US maestros alive today, with a Pulitzer Prize and five GRAMMYs to his name. His numerous operas have dramatised everything from Richard Nixon and Oppenheimer’s nuclear bomb…
